danger_signals.meaning: Direct combat threat; green-colored enemy type signals standard opposition
Escalated danger; major story antagonists present significant threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Four distinct commando characters (Captain Commando primary, plus Mack/Jennety, Ginzu/Sho, Baby/Hoover)
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and support; team composition signals readiness

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy difficulty and boss encounters escalate with stage advancement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character and enemy name changes between Japanese and Western releases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Same mechanical gameplay with altered narrative/aesthetic presentation (censorship in Western version)

environmental_storytelling.example: Four playable commandos with distinct identities (Captain Commando, Mack/Jennety, Ginzu/Sho, Baby/Hoover) establish team composition narrative
Named bosses (Boots, Scumocide, Blood) signal escalating antagonist hierarchy and story beats
Seven-stage progression creates visual/narrative arc of military campaign against enemy organization

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard opposition; green-colored visual identifies threat level as routine
Represent narrative milestones; defeat signals progression to next stage/world state
Four distinct commandos provide tactical variety; character selection signals team configuration

notes: Captain Commando SNES is a port of an arcade game with limited source documentation on visual communication systems. The available sources focus on technical/regional differences rather than world-state manifestation mechanics. Game uses character-driven storytelling (named commando team vs named enemies/bosses) and stage progression system. Western censorship (enemy/boss name changes) indicates cultural/narrative adaptation without mechanical changes. Limited data on environmental visual language, faction color schemes, or detailed danger/safety signal systems.
